content:
The era of user-generated content is over. Now consumer-generated content will define our online world.

5 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

By Ali Babajahdi. (I guess an AI-generated image would have been quite meta)At Google’s annual developer summit last month, CEO Sundar Pichai announced that the company synonymous with online search would no longer be a search engine, but an AI portal that generates answers on command.
Pichai’s announcement ruffled feathers but it was predictable enough. Google referrals are down a third, as people direct their queries to AI services. At the same time, there’s been a noticeable decline in the quality of search engine results as a whole.
This article is part of a series of loosely connected thoughts I have drafted on how LLMs will transform the digital world, that I may or may not publish. We’ll see.
Millennials grew up with a web that was built by hobbyists. For a brief time, it was a rich and diverse market of eccentric, scrappy pages dedicated to the niche interests, uploaded by industrious, loveable weirdos. With full control of the user experience, these sites could — and often did — push the medium of the browser window as far as it could go.
The pinnacle of this era, perhaps, was StumbleUpon, a browser extension that allowed you to jump (thrillingly, magically, randomly) across the web, based on a simple voting system.
StumbleUpon gradually gave way to fixed aggregators: from early linkblog sites such as Metafilter and BoingBoing (which, even if they linked back to the source, sent little traffic that way); and later giant engines Digg and Reddit. These vacuumed up the best of the web, stripped it of context and often ownership, and homogenised the experience of the web to fit into a template that permitted text, images, animation, and little else.
“Web 2.0” might have been characterised by an explosion of user-generated media, but following close behind and in far greater number was the audience — people who logged on to see content, not to make it. Aggregator sites such as Reddit and platforms such as YouTube and Instagram shifted the balance of the web from a place for creators to a place for consumers.
The web still needed the creatives to make interesting things, but now they were reduced to “content creators”. This dreadful term emphasised that their purpose in the new ecosystem was not to explore their own interests, but to provide entertainment for the primary user, the consumer. Instead of making things for themselves, according to their own metrics of success, on their own pages, creatives were expected to adjust their output to what worked best for the platforms where the audiences resided.
And by design — first in manual voting systems and then by algorithm — audiences were shielded from anything that was too niche, unwieldy, unfamiliar, or unflattering, in favour of content that was statistically the most palatable, predictable, compatible, convenient.
The shrinking web
Two decades ago, an evening spent online might have meant visiting 20 or 30 websites. Today, it’s probably no more than three.
Yet despite this massive contraction in our online movements, and the homogeneity of the content on the few sites we do visit, no two users see the same thing. Most of the world spends its screen time on the same handful of sites, and yet each experience is unique. We are online more than ever, and in the same spaces, but more fragmented than ever, with little shared experience across hyper-personalised algorithmic feeds.
Instagram has half a billion daily users and no editorial direction for what it serves up, except what the user wants it to be, as defined in units of attention measured in microseconds. The consumer decides the content, not the creator.
It was inevitable then that the algorithmic feed would eventually shift from finding the most perfectly-aligned content currently available to the user, to creating it on the fly. Amazon’s Alexa will now generate a podcast for you on demand, on whatever topic you ask. The era of user-generated content is over. Now is the era of consumer-generated content.
The main reason that companies such as Instagram have not followed suit, I suspect, is so they can continue to claim that they are a platform and not a publisher, and therefore bear no legal responsibility for material appearing on that site. Plus, they hardly need to.
But it’s not hard to see that in the future, entertainment apps will simply generate content (music, video, chatrooms, pornography) on demand according to the user’s preferences. No longer will a site need a critical mass of creators to generate enough content to maintain user engagement. Most likely the first iterations of this will be styled to look like the platforms of user-generated content we are familiar with. But that will change as we adjust to the idea that we no longer crawl the web, but conjure it.
The consumer-generated web will create itself as we step through it. Search for what you want, and what you want will be created. The shape of the web will shift from all there is to all there could be. And what little shared experience there is will be gone: we will each be alone in the infinite hallways of our personal web.
We’ve been here before. Zazzle is an online marketplace that allows designers to upload their artwork, and customers can then attach these designs to t-shirts, mugs, and posters as they wish.
Sometime around 2010 (you’ll understand my vagueness in a moment) someone at Zazzle had a bright idea. To capture potential customers, the site would create product pages on the fly, based on the input of the search. This way, if someone was to search for an item that didn’t yet exist, it would be made to exist — with a product page and a photo and a price, as if it always had been there. Nobody would ever see the message “Sorry, no items matched your search :(”.
There was just one hitch: there was no filter for the on-the-fly product page. Searching for obscene, offensive or illegal items would result in a product page for those things being created. None of it shipped, because the items didn’t exist, and presumably there was a check somewhere down the line if anyone did order one. But it gave the appearance that Zazzle was selling these items, and that was enough to pull the plug.
Ever since then, I’ve always privately referred to the idea of generating personalised content on-the-fly as a Zazzle-fication process. And the Zazzle-fication of the web would be a great neologism for what is happening.
Except! — I have no evidence that this ever happened. I remember it, as clearly as a middle-aged guy can remember the early-ish web, but there is no trace on Google, and the AI agents feed me half-truths of similar events. My most online friends don’t recall the occurrence. It’s possible that online search is already so degraded and generative that all trace of this episode is gone. Or maybe you don’t need to be an AI to hallucinate a compelling narrative on command. 🤷‍♂️
